role_summary: Co-founder (with husband Rev. Charles Sherrod, catalog entry 18) of New Communities Inc, the 1969 Lee County, Georgia collective farm modeled on the Israeli moshav that became one of the first U.S. community land trusts; longtime Federation of Southern Cooperatives organizer; USDA Georgia State Director of Rural Development (2009-2010); subject of the July 2010 USDA-Breitbart selectively-edited-video firing.
ai_reading: On the catalog's five concept axes, the embedding reads Sherrod's testimony far toward Tactical Pragmatism: at a normalized +0.74 on the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis, only four of the 136 audited entries sit further toward that pole. The position tracks a career spent in the granular fieldwork of cooperative agriculture (business plans, loan regulations, brood-sow operations, feeder-pig training); the vector space registers that day-to-day implementation vocabulary as nearer the tactical pole than the long-arc, vision-and-doctrine register most of the catalog inhabits. The second axis reinforces her biography rather than complicating it: a lifelong southwest-Georgia organizer reads at a normalized +0.67 toward the Southern Struggle pole (only five entries read more Southern), consistent with a transcript rooted in Baker, Lee, and Dougherty county land and the Southwest Georgia Project, even though much of its subject matter is economic-and-institutional (USDA discrimination, land trusts, the Pigford litigation, cooperative finance) rather than the church-and-streets idiom the axis often picks up at the Southern pole. By cosine similarity her nearest neighbor in the corpus is not her husband Charles Sherrod (entry 18) but Sam Young, Jr. (entry 114, count 156), with Linda Fuller Degelmann (entry 78, Habitat for Humanity) and the community-physician H. Jack Geiger (entry 54) also high; the embedding files her with a rural-cooperative-and-economic-development cohort rather than with the Albany Movement voices her biography is usually told alongside.
biographical_paragraph: Shirley Miller Sherrod was born November 20, 1947 in Baker County, Georgia, the eldest of the five daughters of Hosie and Grace Hall Miller; she grew up farming family-owned land in what the community called the Hawkins-family settlement, in a county she describes as run by sheriff L. Warren "Gator" Johnson [src: 1, 5]. In March 1965, during her senior year of high school, her father was shot and killed by a white farmer over a dispute about cattle, and an all-white Baker County grand jury declined to indict the man [src: 5, 4]. She has said that the night of her father's death she resolved to stay in the South and work for change rather than leave for the North as she had planned [src: 1]. She met Charles Sherrod (catalog entry 18), the SNCC field secretary who had opened the Southwest Georgia Project, through the movement that reached Baker County that summer, and the two married about a year later [src: 1, 2]. In 1969 the Sherrods and their co-organizers founded New Communities Inc, a roughly 5,735-acre farm collective in Lee County, Georgia, structured as a land trust after a 1968 study trip to Israel; it is widely cited as one of the first community land trusts in the United States and, at its founding, the largest tract of Black-owned land in the country [src: 2, 4, 6]. After an Office of Economic Opportunity planning grant was blocked by Georgia governor Lester Maddox, and following years of drought and federal lending discrimination, Prudential Insurance foreclosed on the property by 1985 [src: 2, 6]. Sherrod then organized for the Federation of Southern Cooperatives and helped build the litigation that became Pigford v. Glickman; in 2009, ten years after filing, New Communities was awarded roughly $12 million for the discrimination it had documented, resources later used to purchase the 1,638-acre Cypress Pond property near Albany in 2011 [src: 3, 6, 4]. In 2009 she was appointed USDA Georgia State Director of Rural Development, and on July 19, 2010 she was forced to resign after Andrew Breitbart posted a two-minute-and-thirty-eight-second excerpt of a forty-three-minute March 2010 NAACP speech that made a story about overcoming her own early prejudice appear to be an admission of discrimination; the full recording showed the opposite, and Agriculture Secretary Tom Vilsack and the White House apologized within days and offered her another position [src: 7, 8]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 117 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Shirley Miller Sherrod
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing what New Communities was meant to be, she rejected the idea of the founders moving into the plantation's main house:
interview_snippets.quote: We were not trying to recreate something that modeled what was already there. We were actually trying to build something that was new and different and equal.

interview_snippets.speaker: Shirley Miller Sherrod
interview_snippets.lead_in: On what the collectively held land meant, the structure that made New Communities an early community land trust:
interview_snippets.quote: It meant so much to us to have that place, that land, that was owned not by me, not by him, but by us. It was a land trust.

interview_snippets.speaker: Shirley Miller Sherrod
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting on decades of helping both Black and white farmers fight foreclosure, the experience her 2010 NAACP speech recounted:
interview_snippets.quote: And that's why for the last 25 years now I have tried to point that out as much as I could through the years to try to get people here in this area to see that if we can work together we can do more to bring change in this area than we can trying to work against each other.

interview_snippets.speaker: Shirley Miller Sherrod
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how credit for her organizing was attributed, after recounting the pecan-supply arrangement she built with Ben and Jerry's:
interview_snippets.quote: So a lot of my work got you know I'm just out here working trying to bring change not really trying to get the credit for what I did do and in many cases because I was working in organizations where the leadership was a male that work became known as theirs.

interview_snippets.speaker: Grace Hall Miller
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked about her daughter's 2010 USDA firing, Shirley's mother Grace Hall Miller (entry 50) recalled how the family kept the news from her at first:
interview_snippets.quote: I think that was a really hurt thing. The way they treated her.
